claims.flip_evidence_text: has had or would be reasonably likely to have a material adverse effect on (a) the business, operations, condition (financial or otherwise) or results of operations of the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or (b) the ability of the Company to consummate the Transactions

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“Company Material Adverse Effect” means any event, effect, occurrence, fact, circumstance, condition or change that, individually or in the aggregate, has had or would be reasonably likely to have a material adverse effect on (a) the business, operations, condition (financial or otherwise) or results of operations of the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or (b) the ability of the Company to consummate the Transactions; provided, however, that, none of the following shall be deemed in and of themselves, either alone or in combination, to constitute, and except as provided below, none of the following shall be taken into account in determining whether there is, or would reasonably be likely to be, a Company Material Adverse Effect: (i) general economic or political conditions (or changes or disruptions in such conditions) in the United States or any other country or region in the world, or conditions in the global economy generally; (ii) conditions (or changes or disruptions in such conditions) generally affecting the industries in which the Company and Company Subsidiaries operate; (iii) conditions (or changes or disruptions in such conditions) in the securities markets, capital markets, credit markets, currency markets or other financial markets in the United States or any other country or region in the world, including (A) changes in interest rates in the United States or any other country or region in the world and changes in exchange rates for the currencies of any countries and (B) any suspension of trading in equity, debt, derivative or hybrid securities, securities generally (including Company Common Stock) on any securities exchange or over-the-counter market operating in the United States or any other country or region in the world; (iv) political conditions (or changes or disruptions in such conditions) in the United States or any other country or region in the world or acts of war (whether or not declared), armed or unarmed hostilities or attacks, acts of terrorism, sabotage, or the escalation or worsening thereof in the United States or any other country or region in the world; (v) (A) the failure of Parent or Merger Subsidiary to comply with their respective obligations under this Agreement, (B) any actions taken by the Company or the Company Subsidiaries to which Parent has requested or (C) the Company taking any action required by this Agreement; (vi) any changes in applicable Law (including COVID-19 Measures), accounting rules (including GAAP) or other legal or regulatory conditions or the enforcement, implementation or interpretation thereof; (vii) other than for purposes of Section 3.3(c) and Section 3.3(d) (but subject to disclosure in the Company Disclosure Letter for such Sections), the announcement, pendency or completion of this Agreement; (viii) any natural hurricane, earthquake, flood, disaster, acts of God, pandemic (including COVID-19) or other force majeure events in the United States or any other country or region in the world; (ix) changes in the Company’s stock price or the trading volume of the Company’s stock, in and of itself, or any failure by the Company to meet any internal or published forecasts, estimates, projections or expectations of the Company’s revenue, earnings or other financial performance or results of operations for any period (provided that the underlying causes of such changes or failures (subject to the other provisions of this definition) shall not be excluded); (x) any regulatory, preclinical or clinical event, occurrence, circumstance, change, effect or development relating to any Company Product in pre-clinical or clinical research and development (including, for the avoidance of doubt, (A) any test or results or announcements thereof, (B) increased incidence or severity of previously identified side effects, adverse effects, adverse events or safety observations or (C) reports of new side effects, adverse effects, adverse events or safety observations); provided that this clause (x) shall not apply to events, occurrences, circumstances, changes, effects or developments relating to the safety of Belumosudil, which (subject to the other provisions of this definition) shall not be excluded; or (xi) any matters disclosed in the Company Disclosure Letter; except, with respect to clause (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), or (vi) of this definition, to the extent such event, effect, occurrence, fact, circumstance, condition or change disproportionately affects the Company relative to other participants in the industries or geographies in which the Company operates or the economy generally, as applicable. (Pages 75-76)

Question:
Relational language (MAE) applies to

Llama answer:
The definition of "Company Material Adverse Effect" (MAE) applies to events that have a material adverse effect on the business, operations, condition, or results of operations of the Company and its subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or the ability of the Company to consummate the Transactions.

Canonical claims:
- claim-c40933145399: The definition of "Company Material Adverse Effect" (MAE) applies to events that have a material adverse effect on the business, operations, condition, or results of operations of the Company and its subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or the ability of the Company to consummate the Transactions.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. I need to evaluate a claim against a contract excerpt, a question, and a model answer.

Given:
- Contract excerpt: Definition of "Company Material Adverse Effect" (MAE)
- Question: "Relational language (MAE) applies to"
- Llama answer: The definition applies to events that have a material adverse effect on (a) business, operations, condition, or results of operations of the Company and subsidiaries taken as a whole, or (b) the ability of the Company to consummate the Transactions.
- Canonical claims:
  - claim-c40933145399: The definition of "Company Material Adverse Effect" (MAE) applies to events that have a material adverse effect on the business, operations, condition, or results of operations of the Company and its subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or the ability of the Company to consummate the Transactions.

I need to return for each claim:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Let me analyze the claim against the contract text.

Contract text says: "Company Material Adverse Effect" means any event, effect, occurrence, fact, circumstance, condition or change that, individually or in the aggregate, has had or would be reasonably likely to have a material adverse effect on (a) the business, operations, condition (financial or otherwise) or results of operations of the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or (b) the ability of the Company to consummate the Transactions...

The claim states: "The definition of 'Company Material Adverse Effect' (MAE) applies to events that have a material adverse effect on the business, operations, condition, or results of operations of the Company and its subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or the ability of the Company to consummate the Transactions."

This seems to accurately reflect the definition. The claim captures the two main prongs: (a) effect on business/operations/condition/results of Company and subsidiaries taken as a whole, and (b) ability to consummate the Transactions.

Is it fully true? Let me check if there's any nuance missing. The claim says "applies to events" while the contract says "means any event, effect, occurrence, fact, circumstance, condition or change". The claim simplifies this to "events" but that's a reasonable summary. The claim captures the material adverse effect on the two specified areas. It doesn't mention the exceptions/carve-outs, but the claim is specifically about what the definition *applies to* (i.e., the scope of what it covers), not what is excluded. The question is "Relational language (MAE) applies to". So the claim is answering what the MAE definition applies to.

Wait, the claim says "condition, or results of operations". The contract says "condition (financial or otherwise) or results of operations". The claim omits "(financial or otherwise)" but that's minor. It says "Company and its subsidiaries" vs "Company and the Company Subsidiaries". Essentially the same.

So correctness_label should be "true".

Now, load_bearing_label: This is a bit tricky. In legal annotation tasks, "load bearing" usually means whether the specific phrasing of the claim is critical/supported by specific text in the contract, and if flipping/changing it would be evidenced differently. Or it might refer to whether the claim is a "load bearing" structural element of the answer. 

Actually, looking at similar tasks, "load_bearing_label" often asks whether the claim is central/important to the answer and whether there is specific text that supports it (and would need to be "flipped" if the claim were opposite). If load_bearing is yes, I need to provide flip_evidence_text.

But what exactly is the criteria? The prompt says:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Given this is a research pilot and I need to determine load-bearing nature: Usually, a claim is load-bearing if it captures a key substantive point from the text that could be flipped. If the claim is true and directly supported by specific contract language, it's likely load-bearing (yes). The flip_evidence_text would be the text that would support the opposite claim (i.e., if the claim were false, what text would show that? Or is it the text that currently supports the claim, and if flipped, this evidence would be different?).

Wait, "flip_evidence_text" suggests: if the load bearing label is yes, provide the evidence text that would flip the label? Or provide the evidence text from the contract that the claim relies on? Actually, looking at the name "flip_evidence_text", it likely means the textual evidence that would be used if the claim were flipped (i.e., if the claim were false). Or perhaps it means the evidence text that supports the claim, and if the claim were flipped, this text would contradict it. Let me think.

In some annotation schemas, "load_bearing" means that the claim is specific enough that it can be verified against the text. The "flip_evidence_text" is the specific span in the source that either supports or refutes the claim. If the claim is load-bearing, there is specific text that bears the weight of the claim. If you flipped the claim (made it false), you'd need to point to text that contradicts it, or perhaps the same text would be the flip evidence.

Actually, I recall a similar task from legal NLP (e.g., ContractNLI or similar). In those tasks, "load bearing" might refer to whether the claim's truth value depends on specific evidence in the text. If yes, you provide the evidence text. The "flip" might mean: if the claim were negated, this is the text that would need to change/be cited.

Alternatively, it could be that "flip_evidence_text" is simply the evidence text from the contract that supports the claim. But why call it "flip"? Maybe because if the claim is load-bearing, you should provide the text that would "flip" the claim if interpreted differently? No.

Let me reconsider. Perhaps the task is from a "flip evaluation" where for each claim, you identify if it's load-bearing (i.e., if the conclusion depends on it), and then the "flip_evidence_text" is the specific excerpt that would cause the claim to flip from true to false if the text were different. But since we only have the current text, maybe we provide the relevant text span that the claim is based on, and note that if this span were absent or different, the claim would flip.

Given the ambiguity, I'll interpret "flip_evidence_text" as the specific contract text that substantiates (or would flip) the claim. Actually, in many annotation guidelines for legal reasoning, they ask for the "evidence" text. The term "flip" might indicate that if the claim were opposite, this evidence would contradict it. But since the claim is true, the evidence supports it. If we flipped the claim to "does NOT apply to...", this text would refute that flipped claim. So it's the evidence that flips a false claim back to true? Hmm.

Let me look at the term: "flip_evidence_text". Maybe it's the text that you would use to verify the claim. If the claim is load-bearing, there must be a specific span. I'll provide the relevant span from the contract.

The claim is about what the MAE applies to. The relevant text is:
"has had or would be reasonably likely to have a material adverse effect on (a) the business, operations, condition (financial or otherwise) or results of operations of the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or (b) the ability of the Company to consummate the Transactions"

So flip_evidence_text would be that portion.

Is the claim load-bearing? Yes, because it's a substantive claim about the scope of the MAE definition, directly tied to specific language in the contract. If this text weren't there, the claim would be false.

Thus:
- correctness_label: true
- load_bearing_label: yes
- flip_evidence_text: the excerpt above.
- notes: empty or brief confirmation.

Let me double-check if there's any subtlety making it partially_true. The claim says "applies to events". The definition applies to "any event, effect, occurrence, fact, circumstance, condition or change". Is calling all of these "events" inaccurate? In common parlance, "events" is often used as a shorthand. The question is "Relational language (MAE) applies to". The Llama answer captures the two prongs. The claim is a direct quote of the Llama answer. It seems fully supported.

One more thing: the contract says "Company Material Adverse Effect" means... The claim says "The definition... applies to events that have...". This is accurate. The definition indeed captures such things.

Therefore, true.

Wait, I should consider whether the claim omits the carve-outs and the "except" clause, making it partially true. But the claim is specifically about what the definition *applies to*, not what it *excludes*. The contract says it means any [thing] that has a material adverse effect on (a) or (b), provided that none of the following shall be deemed... to constitute... So the definition applies to things that have an MAE on (a) or (b), subject to exclusions. The claim correctly identifies (a) and (b) as the targets of the MAE. It doesn't say "only applies to" or "applies exclusively to". It just says "applies to events that have...". So it's true.

Load bearing: yes, because the claim's truth depends on the specific definitional language in the contract.

Flip evidence text: I'll provide the exact quote from the contract.

Let's format the output. The user wants "For each claim return: ..." and gave one claim. So I'll return a JSON-like or structured output for that claim.

The claim ID is claim-c40933145399.

I should return something like:

claim-c40933145399:
  correctness_label: true
  load_bearing_label: yes
  flip_evidence_text: "has had or would be reasonably likely to have a material adverse effect on (a) the business, operations, condition (financial or otherwise) or results of operations of the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or (b) the ability of the Company to consummate the Transactions"
  notes: ""

Or maybe just a list. The user didn't specify exact format, but bullet points should work. I'll provide clear labels.

Actually, looking at the prompt again: "For each claim return: - correctness_label: ... - load_bearing_label: ... - flip_evidence_text: ... - notes: ..."

I'll present it as:

Claim: claim-c40933145399
- correctness_label: true
- load_bearing_label: yes
- flip_evidence_text: has had or would be reasonably likely to have a material adverse effect on (a) the business, operations, condition (financial or otherwise) or results of operations of the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or (b) the ability of the Company to consummate the Transactions
- notes: ""

Make sure to keep flip_evidence_text exact from the contract excerpt. I'll copy from the provided text.

Provided text: "has had or would be reasonably likely to have a material adverse effect on (a) the business, operations, condition (financial or otherwise) or results of operations of the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or (b) the ability of the Company to consummate the Transactions; provided, however, that, none of the following shall be deemed..."

I should cut at the semicolon before the proviso to keep it focused on the applicability, or include up to the proviso. The proviso doesn't change what it applies to, just what is excluded. The key load-bearing text is the part that defines the two prongs. So I'll use that.

Let's write it cleanly.
